elements.sent-2411-012: The independence axiom is normatively appealing because it is the necessary and sufficient conditions for the expected utility representation, provided that the underlying preference relation is complete, transitive, and continuous.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, many counterexamples in and out of the laboratory, most notably suggested by \textcite{Allais1953 , show that it is possible to systematically violate the axiom, thereby questioning its empirical validity and, thus, that of expected utility theory altogether.}

elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we propose a new middle-ground approach that splits the difference between the normative appeal of expected utility theory and the empirical strengths of prospect theory, but from a novel angle.
elements.sent-2411-018: We first provide and axiomatize a representation of preferences over lotteries that generalizes the expected utility model.
elements.sent-2411-019: A consequence of this generalization is a more flexible utility representation than that offered by prospect theory, cumulative prospect theory, or rank-dependent utility models.
elements.sent-2411-020: To illustrate this idea, consider the following example of preferences over lotteries.
elements.sent-2411-021: Suppose a decision-maker (DM) who faces a risky prospect in which there is a high probability of winning a low prize exhibits the following risk-loving preference, \begin{equation}(\$0, 0.9; \$100, 0.05; \$200, 0.05) \succ (\$0, 0.9; \$150, 0.1),\end{equation} where the value listed after each monetary prize is the probability that the lottery assigns to the prize.
elements.sent-2411-022: Now, suppose we swap the zero in both lotteries with a prize value of $\$90$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The DM is ensured a moderate level of wealth in this case, so the DM becomes risk-averse and exhibits the following preference: \begin{equation}(\$90, 0.9; \$100, 0.05;\$200, 0.05) \prec (\$90,0.9; \$150, 0.1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: Such a change in risk-taking behavior, in which the DM prefers a riskier lottery when the probability of disappointment or doom is large but prefers less risky lotteries when some wealth is guaranteed is similar to what we see in the well-known Allais Paradox.
elements.sent-2411-025: There is a significant divergence, however, from the paradox in that this violation of the independence axiom is not consistent with the aforementioned models of non-expected utility such as the rank-dependent utility of \textcite{Quiggin1982 or the more general cumulative prospect theory of \textcite{Tversky1992 .\footnote{Our example is not consistent with cumulative prospect theory if we let \$0 be the reference point for the model, but we can construct a similar example for any other reference point as well.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: To see why it is not possible to obtain these preferences with the two models, note that the worst prize in both lotteries is \$0 at probability 0.9 in both.

elements.sent-2411-029: For the lottery on the left side, \$200 remains the best prize and \$100 remains the second best in the lottery after the swap, and \$0/\$90 is the worst prize before/after the swap.

elements.sent-2411-100: We start this section by introducing the setup for the model.
elements.sent-2411-101: We let $X=[w,b]\subset \mathbb{R}$, where $w<b$, represent a set of prizes.
elements.sent-2411-102: Endow $X$ and $\mathbb{R}$ with a metric topology where the metric is $dist(x,y)=|x-y|$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The set of Borel probability measures with finite support on $X$, which we also call simple lotteries, is denoted as $\cal{L}$.
elements.sent-2411-104: For any $x\in X$, $\delta_x$ is the Dirac measure at $x$.
elements.sent-2411-105: We let $\succsim$ be a binary preference relation over $\cal{L}$.
elements.sent-2411-106: We define the threshold for what the DM consideres as disappointing prizes as in \textcite{Honda2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The value $d\in [w,b]$ is the threshold for the DM's disappointment with a prize, so prizes in $[w,d]$ are disappointing.
elements.sent-2411-108: The utility functions that the DM uses to evaluate a lottery depends on the probability associated with disappointing prizes, or prizes that are less than or equal to $d$.
elements.sent-2411-109: Thus, for each $\pi\in (0,1)$, we have a function $u_\pi:X\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that can be interpreted as a utility function over prizes.
elements.sent-2411-110: If a lottery $p\in \cal{L}$ assigned probability $\pi\in(0,1)$ to prizes in $[w,d]$ or $p([w,d])=\pi$, the DM uses the utility function $u_{\pi}$.
elements.sent-2411-111: This allows for changes in risk attitudes based on a lottery's context.
elements.sent-2411-112: We can thus generate behaviors like those mentioned in the introduction with risk-lovingness for high probabilities of disappointment and risk-aversion for low probabilities.

elements.sent-2411-612: We recruited 95 student participants from a participant pool at the Economic Science Institute (ESI) Laboratory at Chapman University in Orange, California.
elements.sent-2411-613: We conducted five experimental sessions, each lasting approximately 30 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-614: These sessions included instructions that participants read before the main experiment and a non-incentivized questionnaire upon completion of the main experiment.
elements.sent-2411-615: In the first two sessions, we allowed participants to switch freely between the two columns in Stages 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-616: In the three later sessions the participants were allowed to switch only once in each stage.
elements.sent-2411-617: For all five sessions, participants received a \$7 show-up fee.
elements.sent-2411-618: The dollar amounts they could earn for each option varied from \$0 to a maximum of \$24 in all except the second session.
elements.sent-2411-619: Hence, the total amount they could earn ranged from \$7 to \$31.
elements.sent-2411-620: In the second session, the amount they could earn ranged from \$7 to \$103 for the 30-minute session.
elements.sent-2411-621: No participant participated in more than one session.
elements.sent-2411-622: The overall results obtained from the pilot sessions are qualitatively consistent with our main findings.
elements.sent-2411-623: We list them separately because of two main differences in the sessions.
elements.sent-2411-624: First, the pilot sessions were all conducted in a physical lab environment whereas the main data were collected in an online environment.
elements.sent-2411-625: Second, the pilot sessions were all conducted using dollar amounts, whereas the online experiments used points as the main denomination.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Pilot sessions with multiple switches allowed

elements.sent-2411-627: While this represents a natural design when testing the validity of our model, the data show that the majority of participants switched multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-628: Most importantly, these multiple switches obstructed effective estimation of the model parameters.

elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{./result_stage12_pilot.png} \caption{The violation of EU/RDU/CPT appears is more pronounced in the pilot sessions.} \label{fig:result_stage12_pilot}
elements.sent-2411-649: In particular, 37 of 42 (88.1\%) participants who switched in Stage 1 did so in Stage 2.
elements.sent-2411-650: Of the 25 participants who did not switch in Stage 1, only 12 (48.0\%) switched in Stage 2.
elements.sent-2411-651: The correlation between participants' behavior in Stages 1 and 2 suggests as well that reversals in risk attitudes over the two stages are individual-specific, indicating the plausibilityh of our intuition that thresholds on both the prize and probability dimensions explain the results.
elements.sent-2411-652: In the set of choice tasks where our model predicted a lack of Allais behavior: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-653: The empirically observed switching frequency in the Allais-predicted sets of choice tasks is consistent with findings reported by \textcite{Blavatskyy2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-654: That meta-study reports that approximately 15\% of experiments using real incentives reveal fanning-out, while 20\% reveal fanning-in, in contrast to the much higher frequencies reported in experiments employing hypothetical incentives with large award amounts, as in \textcite{Kahneman1979 .. .}

elements.sent-2411-655: Welcome to the experiment! Thank you very much for participating today.
elements.sent-2411-656: This research is conducted by researchers of the University of Manitoba, Canada and Fairfield University, USA.
elements.sent-2411-657: The experiment you will be participating in is an experiment in individual decision making.
elements.sent-2411-658: You will receive your compensation via Prolific.
elements.sent-2411-659: You will receive the show-up fee of \$6.00 for completing the experiment, with the additional bonus amount that depends on your decisions and on chance.
elements.sent-2411-660: The details of the compensation will be described later.
elements.sent-2411-661: All instructions and descriptions that you will be given in the experiment are accurate and true.
elements.sent-2411-662: At no point will we attempt to deceive you in any way.
elements.sent-2411-663: When you are ready, please click the button below to read the instructions.
elements.sent-2411-664: There are three parts in this experiment.
elements.sent-2411-665: First, there is a short quiz at the end of the instructions to ensure your understanding of the procedures.
elements.sent-2411-666: You will be able to repeat the quiz if you make mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-667: You will have five chances to attempt the quiz.
elements.sent-2411-668: If you fail to get all questions correct after five attempts, you may not participate in the main experiment.
elements.sent-2411-669: If you are unable to pass the quiz, please understand that we can only compensate those who participate in the main part of the experiment and we will not be able to provide payment.
elements.sent-2411-670: In such an event, we kindly request that you return your submission on Prolific.
elements.sent-2411-671: In the main part of the experiment, we will ask you to perform tasks of choosing between two options.
elements.sent-2411-672: We will shortly explain what they are.
elements.sent-2411-673: At the end of the experiment, we will display a review screen to verify your payment information.
elements.sent-2411-674: We will now give you more details about the main experiment.
elements.sent-2411-675: The basic unit of the experiment is a stage of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-676: Each stage consists of one or more tasks.
elements.sent-2411-677: In each stage, we ask you to perform a series of tasks of choosing between two available options.
elements.sent-2411-678: Each option in a task represents a lottery by which you can earn certain amounts of points depending on chances.
elements.sent-2411-679: Below is an example of such options.
elements.sent-2411-680: The columns and the image on the left represent a hypothetical Option A that gives you 150 points with 50\% chance and 0 points with 50\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-681: The columns and the image on the right represent a hypothetical Option B that gives you 200 points with 10\% chance, 150 points with 30\% chance, and 0 points with 60\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-682: Your task is to indicate which of the two options you prefer by clicking the arrow buttons located between the two lotteries.
elements.sent-2411-683: The combination of options and the chances associated with them vary across tasks.
elements.sent-2411-684: The amount of points you may earn in each Option varies from 0 to the maximum of 800.
elements.sent-2411-685: The chances associated with them vary from 0\% to 100\%.
elements.sent-2411-686: There are three stages in today’s experiment.
elements.sent-2411-687: In Stage 1 and Stage 2, we present a table that contains a series of ten (10) tasks.
elements.sent-2411-688: Each row represents a task of choosing between Option A and Option B.
elements.sent-2411-689: In Stage 1, we will fix the chances and vary the amounts of points you can receive in each Option across the ten rows.
elements.sent-2411-690: In Stage 2, we will fix the amounts of points you can receive and vary the chances associated with them across the ten rows.
elements.sent-2411-691: In Stages 1 and 2, we will ask you to simply indicate a threshold line above which you would rather have Option A and below which you would rather have Option B, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-692: You can do so by clicking one of the arrows displayed in the center column of the table.
elements.sent-2411-693: Once you make your choice, a confirmation prompt will appear at the bottom of the screen to remind you of your selection.
elements.sent-2411-694: This will simplify the experiment and reduce the amount of repetitive procedures you need to complete.
elements.sent-2411-695: By doing so, you will only need to perform this procedure once instead of ten times for each row.
elements.sent-2411-696: In Stage 3, we will present one task at a time.
elements.sent-2411-697: There will be eight (8) tasks in Stage 3, and we will vary both the amounts of points you can receive and the chances associated with them.
elements.sent-2411-698: Combining all three Stages, there are total of 28 tasks in today’s experiment.
elements.sent-2411-699: After completing all 28 tasks, the computer will randomly select one task for the payment.
elements.sent-2411-700: Each task has an equal chance of being selected.
elements.sent-2411-701: The final amount you receive will depend on your choice in the randomly chosen task.
elements.sent-2411-702: The computer will simulate a lottery draw based on the option of your choice.
elements.sent-2411-703: For example, if the option of your choice were Option A in the hypothetical example, you will receive 150 points with 50\% chance and 0 points with 50\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-704: For your convenience, imagine this drawing process as the following.
elements.sent-2411-705: Suppose there is a box that contains 50 red balls, and 50 yellow balls.
elements.sent-2411-706: The computer shuffles the box and take one ball out at random.
elements.sent-2411-707: If the ball is red, you receive 150 points, hence 50\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-708: If the ball is yellow, you receive 0 points, hence 50\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-709: If the option of your choice were Option B, on the other hand, you will receive 200 points with 10\% chance, 150 points with 30\% chance, and 0 points with 60\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-710: Suppose there is a box that contains 10 red balls, 30 yellow balls, and 60 green balls.
elements.sent-2411-711: If the ball is red, you receive 200 points, hence 10\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-712: If the ball is yellow, you receive 150 points, hence 30\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-713: If the ball is green, you receive 0 points, hence 60\% chance.
elements.sent-2411-715: That is, each point you receive is worth \$0.01.
elements.sent-2411-716: This is the end of the instructions.
elements.sent-2411-717: You may use the 'Previous' button to read the previous pages.
elements.sent-2411-718: Please make sure you understand the instructions well before starting the quiz.
elements.sent-2411-719: When you are ready, please click the next button to start the quiz.
